title: US Launches Second Night of Strikes on Iran as Ceasefire Unravels, NATO Pledges Billions, Le Pen Defies Court
subhead: The US hit Iran's southern coast for a second consecutive night, striking Chabahar and IRGC naval bases after Tehran attacked commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. At the NATO summit in Ankara, allies announced a £37bn missile programme and €70bn for Ukraine, while Marine Le Pen launched a presidential campaign hours after a court upheld her embezzlement conviction.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran Again as Ceasefire Crumbles
sections.body:
US Central Command launched a second round of strikes on Iran overnight, hitting targets along the country's southern coast including the port city of Chabahar — struck for the first time in the conflict — and the IRGC Navy's Imam Ali base. Iranian state media reported explosions in Konarak, Bandar Abbas, and on the disputed islands of Abu Musa and Sirik. Air defence sirens also sounded in Bahrain, where Iran's IRGC had earlier retaliated with missile and drone attacks on US military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait.

President Trump declared the ceasefire memorandum with Iran was 'over', calling Iran's leaders 'scum' and 'sick people'. He told reporters aboard Air Force One: 'Every time they hit us, we're going to hit them 20.' An unnamed US official told Reuters the latest strikes were wider in scope than Tuesday's round, which hit over 80 targets including more than 60 IRGC small boats, air defence systems, and coastal radar. Trump later described the conflict not as a war but as 'de-nuclearisation of Iran'.

Oil prices rose 1% on the exchanges, with Brent crude climbing to around $78.80 a barrel — still well below the near-$120 highs seen during earlier stages of the war, but reversing the dip that followed June's interim deal.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-Iran ceasefire, barely three weeks old, is functionally dead. Five commercial vessels have been attacked in the Strait of Hormuz since the June 17 deal, and the US is now conducting sustained military operations against Iranian territory. The key question is whether this escalates into a full resumption of war or settles into a pattern of proportional strikes and counterstrikes that both sides tolerate.

sections.heading: NATO Summit: Missiles, Money, and Rifts in Ankara
sections.body:
The two-day NATO summit in Ankara closed with major commitments — and exposed fractures. Twelve countries, led by the UK, announced the Deep Precision Strike project, a £37bn ($50bn) programme to develop long-range missiles capable of hitting targets up to 1,250 miles away. NATO also pledged €70bn ($80bn) in military assistance, equipment, and training for Ukraine, with a commitment to sustain at least equivalent levels in 2027.

President Trump used the summit to attack allies who refused to support the Iran war. 'I'm very upset with NATO,' he said, singling out Spain as 'a wasted cause' and instructing Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to 'cut it off' from trade. He also revived his push to acquire Greenland, telling reporters the Danish territory was 'not important for Denmark' — prompting rebuttals from Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen and the EU. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte defended the strikes on Iran as 'absolutely necessary' and insisted the alliance was unified.

On defence spending, only five of NATO's 32 members are projected to meet the 3.5% GDP core spending target in 2026. Trump said several countries had 'truly answered the call' but pressed for more, reiterating his demand for 5% of GDP on defence and security by 2035.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit showed NATO can mobilise significant resources — £37bn for a new missile and €70bn for Ukraine — while simultaneously struggling with internal cohesion. Trump's attacks on Spain and his Greenland remarks underscore that the alliance's biggest fracture is between Washington and its European partners, not between NATO and its adversaries.

sections.heading: Trump Offers Ukraine Patriot Missile Licence as Russia's Fuel Crisis Bites
sections.body:
At the Ankara summit, Trump told Zelensky the US would 'give you a licence to make Patriots', referring to the Patriot interceptor missile system. Ukraine has been asking for licensed domestic production since May, as its existing interceptor stockpiles are critically depleted — the Ukrainian Air Force said none of the 23 ballistic missiles Russia fired on Sunday night were shot down, killing more than 20 people. The US itself has limited stocks, having used more than half its Patriot inventory during the Iran war, according to CSIS.

Military experts expressed scepticism about Ukraine's ability to manufacture Patriot missiles domestically given their complexity and the fact that no part of Ukrainian territory is safe from Russian strikes. Former security officer Ivan Stupak told the BBC production would more likely take place on European soil under supervision.

Meanwhile, Russia is grappling with a deepening domestic fuel crisis. BBC reporting from Moscow showed long queues at petrol stations, rationing across multiple regions, and even Cossacks deployed to keep order at fuel lines in Anapa. Ukrainian drone and missile strikes on Russian oil refineries have compounded the problem. Putin addressed the shortages on state TV, insisting the situation was 'not critical' while his government subsidised fuel prices and authorised lower-grade fuel sales. Independent polling by Levada Center shows Putin's approval dipping to 74%, with only 52% of Russians saying the country is heading in the right direction — down from 61% in May.
sections.whyItMatters: The Patriot licence is a symbolic move more than an immediate military solution — production would take months even on European soil. But Russia's fuel crisis is a real and growing pressure point. The question, as the BBC's reporting underscores, is whether economic pain translates into political pressure on Putin. Evidence so far suggests Russians are frustrated but resigned, not rebellious.

sections.heading: Marine Le Pen Launches Presidential Campaign Despite Embezzlement Conviction
sections.body:
Marine Le Pen confirmed she is running for the French presidency in 2027 just hours after a Paris appeals court upheld her guilty verdict for embezzling €4.1m in public funds. The court, however, reduced her ban on holding public office from five years to one — a crucial modification that allows her to campaign while she appeals to France's highest court, the Court of Cassation. Le Pen immediately launched an online campaign titled 'La Renaissance' — a deliberate jab at President Macron's party of the same name.

Legal experts say Le Pen's strategy is a gamble: if the Court of Cassation rules quickly, she could be forced to wear an electronic tag during the final weeks of the campaign, a reminder of her conviction. But if proceedings stretch, she could potentially win the presidency and claim immunity for a full five-year term. Her nationalist platform — 'The People' versus 'The Metropolitan Elite' — resonates in a France where traditional parties have lost credibility. Polls suggest she has a credible path to the Élysée, though she is not the favourite.

The stakes extend well beyond France. Le Pen and her protégé Jordan Bardella are Eurosceptic and sceptical of both NATO and military support for Ukraine. A Le Pen presidency would shift France's posture on European security at a time when the continent is rearming in response to Russia.
sections.whyItMatters: A convicted embezzler launching a presidential campaign is not normal politics. But the court's decision to shorten her ban — rather than uphold it — has given Le Pen a path to run. If she wins, France's position on NATO, Ukraine, and EU defence coordination would shift dramatically, with consequences for the entire European security order.

sections.heading: Syria Delisted as State Sponsor of Terrorism; ICC Advances Sudan War Crimes Probe
sections.body:
The US announced it will remove Syria from the state sponsor of terrorism list after 47 years, effective in 45 days unless Congress blocks the move. Secretary of State Marco Rubio cited 'formal assurances' from Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa that Syria 'will not support acts of international terrorism in the future'. Trump, meeting al-Sharaa on the sidelines of the NATO summit, said he was 'doing an unbelievable job in unifying Syria'. The delisting removes the primary legal barrier to US and international investment in Syria's reconstruction. Only Iran, North Korea, and Cuba will remain on the list.

Separately, the ICC's deputy chief prosecutor Nazhat Shameem Khan told the BBC the court had reached a 'breakthrough' in its Sudan war crimes investigation, with 'concrete evidence' linking RSF leaders to atrocities in el-Fasher and el-Geneina. The announcement comes as el-Obeid, a city of 500,000 in central Sudan, faces an escalating drone assault by RSF forces — 27 strikes in June alone, according to monitoring group Acled. UN human rights chief Volker Turk warned that el-Obeid could face violence on the scale of el-Fasher, where more than 6,000 people were killed in three days. The city has been under siege-like conditions for 18 months, with fuel, water, and electricity all under threat.
sections.whyItMatters: The Syria delisting is a calculated bet by the Trump administration that al-Sharaa can stabilise the country — and a signal to Israel, which has continued striking Syrian targets, that Washington's priorities have shifted. In Sudan, the ICC breakthrough is significant, but accountability moves far slower than the atrocities it documents. El-Obeid's plight shows how the world's worst humanitarian crises can grind on with minimal international intervention.

sections.heading: Typhoon Maysak Devastates Southern China
sections.body:
Typhoon Maysak, the first to make landfall in China in the 2026 season, has killed at least 21 people, injured hundreds, and displaced tens of thousands across Guangxi and Hubei provinces. In Guangxi's Nanning region, floodwaters submerged entire communities, with residents describing water rising to knee-height within hours. At least 60,000 people have been evacuated and 90,000 affected, according to state media.

The typhoon also triggered rare tornadoes in Hubei province, hundreds of kilometres inland — a phenomenon meteorologists attributed to cold northern air colliding with warm air from Maysak. In Huanggang, a man was sucked from his 12th-floor apartment by tornado-force winds and remains in intensive care. Students described being hit by flying glass as dormitory windows shattered.

President Xi Jinping ordered 'all-out' rescue operations. A second storm, Super Typhoon Bavi, is churning across the Pacific toward China's eastern coast. Residents in affected areas reported limited rescue capacity, families trapped without food or communication, and escaped snakes from flooded farms adding to the hazards.
sections.whyItMatters: Two typhoons in quick succession, with one producing inland tornadoes far from the coast, underscores how climate-linked extreme weather is stretching China's disaster response and hitting its agriculture-dependent economy. With Super Typhoon Bavi approaching, the disruption is not over.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire is effectively over after two consecutive nights of American strikes on Iranian territory and Iranian retaliatory attacks on Gulf bases — and the chances of a long-term agreement now look slimmer than they have since the war began. At NATO, allies mobilised significant resources (£37bn for a new missile, €70bn for Ukraine) but the alliance's internal fractures were on full display, with Trump attacking Spain, threatening trade cuts, and reviving his Greenland ambition. France's Le Pen launched a presidential campaign despite a corruption conviction that should have ended her career — a reminder that the European political landscape remains volatile. In Syria, the US removed a 47-year terrorism designation, betting on a former rebel leader's ability to rebuild a shattered country. And in Sudan and China, humanitarian crises continued to escalate with no end in sight.
